When God gave Moses the blueprint for the tabernacle, He wasn't merely providing architectural plans—He was teaching Israel something profound about holiness, access, and His own nature. Every measurement, every material, every deliberate detail served a purpose. The ten finely woven curtains with their intricate cherubim weren't decorative flourishes but visual reminders that God's dwelling place existed in a different category from ordinary space. The blue, purple, and scarlet colors signified royalty and redemption. This wasn't a structure anyone could wander into casually. It was constructed with intention, guarded with barriers, and built to communicate a central truth: God is present, but His presence demands reverence.

Notice how the chapter moves from the innermost sanctuary outward—the veil separating the Most Holy Place where God's presence dwelt, the Holy Place where His service was conducted, the tent that housed it all, and finally the outer courtyard. Each layer created distance, each boundary mattered. The veil itself, woven with cherubim like those guarding Eden's gates, stood as a physical manifestation of the separation between fallen humanity and the holy God. Only once a year, only the high priest, only with blood. This wasn't cruelty; it was mercy. A God who is truly holy cannot coexist casually with sin. The tabernacle acknowledged this reality in wood and fabric and precious metals.

Yet embedded in these architectural details is a problem that will haunt all of Scripture until it reaches its resolution in Christ. The very structure that testified to God's presence also testified to humanity's distance from it. All these frames and crossbars and curtains and clasps—they were necessary because sinners cannot stand before a holy God. The elaborate system of sacrifices, the restricted access, the mediating priests—they were all saying something we need to hear: your sin has created a chasm, and you cannot bridge it yourself. But there's a tension here that the text doesn't resolve. The tabernacle made provision for approach, even if limited. It said God wanted His people near, even if not near enough to touch His holiness directly. This longing for closeness, held in tension with the inability to achieve it, is what makes the gospel so staggering when it comes.

When you read that the veil would "separate the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place," you're reading about your own condition in the old covenant framework—separated, outside, unable to enter. But that veil hanging in the darkness of the Holy of Holies becomes the central symbol of what Christ would accomplish. When Matthew records that the veil tore from top to bottom at Jesus's death, he's not simply describing a physical event. He's announcing that the separation has been torn away, that access that was impossible has become reality, that the God whose holiness once required barriers now invites you into His presence through the blood of His Son. The tabernacle's intricate beauty and its elaborate restrictions were preparing you to understand the magnitude of what it cost for those restrictions to be removed. Look at this chapter not just as ancient architecture but as a mirror of your own need, and then let it drive you to gratitude for a mercy that reaches through the torn veil.
